title: Calculating opportunity costs from a production possibilities curve (PPC)
description: In this exercise, practice using data from a production possibilities curve to calculate opportunity costs.
topic_path: Calculating opportunity costs from a production possibilities curve (PPC)

assessment_items.question_content:
The production possibilities curves (PPCs) for Jeff and Mita are shown here:

Jeff’s PPC | Mita’s PPC
-|-
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d20b0b52b1449fae3ee5c1df0f904538774af7bb) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0e5c65359840375210ac9a555a245d01f0ddfd8)

**What happens to each producer’s opportunity cost of fresh baked bread as more bread is produced?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jeff’s increases; Mita’s stays the same
Jeff’s stays the same; Mita’s stays the same

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the production possibilities of Country X for two goods. 

Apples (tons)| Wool (bales)|
- | - 
$500$ | $0$
$400$ | $4{,}000$ 
$300$ | $8{,}000$
$200$ | $12{,}000$
$100$ | $16{,}000$
$0$| $20{,}000$

**What is the opportunity cost of one ton of apples in Country X?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Country X always gives up $4{,}000$ bales of wool when it produces another $100$ tons of apples:

$\begin{align}20{,}000 - 16{,}000 &= 4{,}000 &&\small\gray{\text{Bales of wool given up going from no apples to 100 tons of apples}}\\\\
16{,}000 - 12{,}000& = 4{,}000 &&\small\gray{\text{Bales of wool given up going from 100 tons of apples to 200 apples}}\\\\
12{,}000 - 18{,}000 &= 4{,}000 &&\small\gray{\text{Bales of wool given up going from 200 tons of apples to 300 apples}}\\\\
8{,}000 - 4{,}000 &= 4{,}000 &&\small\gray{\text{Bales of wool given up going from 300 tons of apples to 400 apples}}\\\\
4{,}000 - 0 &= 4{,}000 &&\small\gray{\text{Bales of wool given up going from 400 tons of apples to 500 apples}} \end{align}$
We can find the opportunity cost of each of these $100$ tons of apples by dividing the opportunity cost of $100$ apples by $100$:

$\begin{align} 100 \text{ tons of apples} &=4{,}000 \text{ bales of wool}\\\\
\dfrac{100 \text{ tons of apples}}{\blueD{100}} &= \dfrac{4{,}000 \text{ bales of wool}}{\blueD{100}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide each side by 100}}\\\\
1 \text{ ton of apples}&=40 \text{ bales of wool}\end{align}$


The opportunity cost of $1$ ton of apples is $40$ bales of wool.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the production possibilities of Country X for two goods. 

Apples (tons)| Wool (bales)|
- | - 
$500$ | $0$
$400$ | $4{,}000$ 
$300$ | $8{,}000$
$200$ | $12{,}000$
$100$ | $16{,}000$
$0$| $20{,}000$

**What is the opportunity cost of one bale of wool in Country X?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Country X always gives up $100$ tons of apples when it produces another $4{,}000$ bales of wool:

$\begin{align}500-400&= 100 &&\small\gray{\text{Tons of apples given up going from no wool to 4,000 bales of wool}}\\\\
400-300&= 100 &&\small\gray{\text{Tons of apples given up going from 4,000 bales of wool to 8,000 bales}}\\\\
300-200&= 100 &&\small\gray{\text{Tons of apples given up going from 8,000 bales of wool to 12,000 bales}}\\\\
200-100&= 100 &&\small\gray{\text{Tons of apples given up going from 12,000 bales of wool to 16,000 bales}}\\\\
100-0&= 100 &&\small\gray{\text{Tons of apples given up going from 16,000 bales of wool to 20,000 bales}}\end{align}$
We can find the opportunity cost of each of these $4{,}000$ bales of wool by dividing the opportunity cost of $4{,}000$ bales of wool by $4{,}000$:

$\begin{align} 4{,}000 \text{ bales of wool} &=100 \text{ tons of apples}\\\\
\dfrac{4{,}000 \text{ bales of wool}}{\blueD{4{,}000}} &= \dfrac{100  \text{ tons of apples}}{\blueD{4{,}000}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide each side by} 4{,}000}\\\\
1 \text{ bales of wool}&=0.025 \text{ tons of apples}\end{align}$
The opportunity cost of $1$ bale of wool is $0.025$ a ton of apples.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below represents points along Country Z's production possibilities frontier.


Point on PPC| Good A  | Good B | 
- | - | -
M| $400$ | $0$ 
N|$350$ | $100$
O|$300$ | $200$ 
P|$250$ | $300$ 

**What is country Z’s opportunity cost of each unit of good A if it increases production from $300$ units of good A to $350$ units of good A?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Every time Country Z produces 50 more units of Good A, it gives up producing 100 units of good B:

$\begin{align} 100-0 &=100 &&\small\gray{\text{Units of good B given up to make } 50\text{ more of good A}}\\\\
200-100&=100 &&\small\gray{\text{Units of good B given up to make } 50\text{ more of good A}}\\\\
300-200&=100 &&\small\gray{\text{Units of good B given up to make } 50\text{ more of good A}}
\end{align}$

To find the opportunity cost of each of the 50 units of good A, divide the number of good B given up by the number of good B gained:

$\begin{align} 50 \text{ good A gained} &=100 \text{ good B given up}\\\\
\dfrac{50 \text{ good A gained}}{\blueD{50}} & = \dfrac{100 \text{ good B given up}}{\blueD{50}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide each side by} 50}\\\\
1 \text{ good A gained}&=2 \text{ good B given up}\end{align}$

The answer:

> The opportunity cost of each unit of good A is 2 units of good B.

assessment_items.question_content:
The production possibilities curve shown here indicates Theo’s tradeoffs when he produces model cars or scary masks. He is currently using all of his resources efficiently.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/675001456f10b3623cd6ee8e943d90cf308fb930)

**What is the opportunity cost of a scary mask?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Theo’s PPC is a straight line, so his opportunity cost of a scary mask is the same no matter how many he is currently producing.
Since he has constant opportunity costs, you can use the two extremes of production to find his opportunity costs. According to this PPC, he can produce $16$ scary masks (and zero model cars) or $0$ scary masks (and $20$ model cars). 
Mathematically, this can be expressed as:

$16 \text{ Scary masks} = 20 \text{ Model cars}$
To find the opportunity cost of a scary mask, solve for 1 scary mask:

$\begin{align} 16 \text{ Scary masks} &= 20 \text{ Model cars} \\\\
\dfrac{16 \text{ Scary masks} }{\blueD{16}} &=\dfrac{20 \text{ Model cars} }{\blueD{16}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide both sides by 16}}\\\\
1 \text{ Scary mask} &=\dfrac{20}{\blueD{16}}\text{ Model cars}\\\\
1 \text{ Scary mask} &=\dfrac{5}{\blueD{4}}\text{ Model cars} &&\small\gray{\text{Simplify the fraction}}\\\\
1 \text{ Scary mask} &=1.25 \text{ Model cars}
\end{align}$
The answer:

>The opportunity cost of a scary mask is 1.25 model cars.

choices.content: It depends on where Theo is currently producing

assessment_items.question_content:
The figure below is the production possibilities curve showing production of T-Rex costumes and Velociraptor costumes in a single day.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e032afe5f6082da91f3e31cf0dec2ad23e50f396)

**Based on the production possibilities curve shown here, what is the opportunity cost of a T-Rex costume?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This PPC is a straight line, so the opportunity cost of a T-Rex costume is the same no matter how many are produced.
According to this PPC, $10$ T-Rex costumes (and 0 Velociraptor costumes) can be produced or $0$ T-Rex costumes (and 40 Velociraptor costumes) can be produced. 
Mathematically, this can be expressed as:

$10 \text{ T-Rex costumes} = 40 \text{ Velociraptor costumes}$
To find the opportunity cost of a T-Rex costume, solve for 1 T-Rex costume:

$\begin{align} 10 \text{ T-Rex costumes} &= 40 \text{ Velociraptor costumes} \\\\
\dfrac{10 \text{ T-Rex costumes} }{\blueD{10}} &=\dfrac{40 \text{ Velociraptor costumes} }{\blueD{10}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide both sides by 10}}\\\\
1 \text{ T-Rex costume} &=4\text{ Velociraptor costumes} 
\end{align}$
The answer:

> The opportunity cost of a T-Rex costume is $4$ Velociraptor costumes.

assessment_items.question_content:
This production possibilities curve (PPC) shows the tradeoffs faced when deciding on whether to produce bannocks or bowls of skirlie. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/04c49404097ee1a635abcd752aa799eba0083277)

What is the opportunity cost of a bowl of skirlie?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This PPC is a straight line, so the opportunity cost of a skirlie is the same no matter how many are produced.
According to this PPC, 12 bowls of skirlie (and 0 bannocks) can be produced *or* 0 bowls of skirlie (and 4 bannocks) can be produced.
Mathematically, this can be expressed as:

$12 \text{ skirlie} = 4 \text{ bannocks}$

To find the opportunity cost of a skirlie, solve for 1 skirlie:

$\begin{align} 12 \text{ skirlie} &= 4 \text{ bannocks} \\\\
\dfrac{12 \text{ skirlie} }{\blueD{12}} &=\dfrac{4 \text{ bannocks} }{\blueD{12}} &&\small\gray{\text{Divide both sides by 12}}\\\\
1 \text{ skirlie} &=\dfrac{4}{12} \text{ bannocks} \\\\
1 \text{ skirlie} &=\dfrac{1}{3} \text{ bannocks} &&\small\gray{\text{simplify the fraction}}
\end{align}$
The answer:

The opportunity cost of a bowl of skirlie is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a bannock.

assessment_items.question_content:
The production possibilities curve shown here illustrates how many tasty bowls of colcannon and delicious plates of boxty that can be made in a single day.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/241281a0ef6015cc8221ca7c0054d7a75c369495)

**Based on the production possibilities curve (PPC) shown here, what is the opportunity cost of a bowl of colcannon when a producer increases colcannon production from $5$ bowls to $8$ bowls?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When $5$ bowls of colcannon are produced, a producer can also make $11$ plates of boxty. But when $8$ bowls of colcannon are produced, a producer can only make $9$ plates of boxty.
A producer gives up $2$ plates of boxty to gain $3$ bowls of colcannon when these quantities are produced:

$\begin{align} \text{Bowls of colcannon gained} & = 8-5\\\\
&= 3\\\\
\text{ Plates of boxty lost}
& = 11-9\\\\
&=2\end{align}$
This can be expressed by the equation shown here:

$3 \text{ Bowls of colcannon} = 2\text{ Plates of boxty}$
To find the opportunity cost of a single bowl of colcannon, solve for 1 bowl of colcannon:

$\begin{align} 3\text{ Bowls of colcannon}&=2\text{ Plates of boxty}\\\\
\dfrac{3 \text{ Bowls of colcannon}}{\blueD{3}}&=\dfrac{2 \text{ Plates of boxty}}{\blueD{3}}&&\small\gray{\text{Divide each side by 3}}\\\\
1 \text{ Bowl of colcannon} &= \dfrac{2}{3}\text{ Plates of boxty}\end{align}$
The opportunity cost of a bowl of colcannon is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of a plate of boxty.

assessment_items.question_content:
The production possibilities curve shown here illustrates how many tasty bowls of colcannon and delicious plates of boxty that can be made in a single day.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0908581b458ce98d3043df7362ae91c4e5f699f9)


**Based on the production possibilities curve (PPC) shown here, what is the opportunity cost of a bowl of colcannon when a producer increases colcannon production from $9$ bowls to $11$ bowls?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When $9$ bowls of colcannon are produced, a producer can also make $8$ plates of boxty. But when $11$ bowls of colcannon are produced, a producer can only make $5$ plates of boxty.
A producer gives up $3$ plates of boxty to gain $2$ bowls of colcannon:

$\begin{align} \text{ Bowls of colcannon gained} & = 11-9\\\\
&= 2\\\\
\text{ Plates of boxty lost} & = 8-5\\\\
&=3\end{align}$
This can be expressed by the equation shown here:

$2 \text{ Bowls of colcannon} = 3\text{ Plates of boxty}$
To find the opportunity cost of a single bowl of colcannon, solve for 1 bowl of colcannon:
$\begin{align} 2\text{ Bowls of colcannon}&=3\text{ Plates of boxty}\\\\
\dfrac{2 \text{ Bowls of colcannon}}{\blueD{2}}&=\dfrac{3 \text{ Plates of boxty}}{\blueD{2}}&&\small\gray{\text{Divide each side by 2}}\\\\
1 \text{ Bowl of colcannon} &= \dfrac{3}{2}\text{ Plates of boxty}\end{align}$
The opportunity cost of a bowl of colcannon is $\dfrac{3}{2}$, or 1.5, plates of boxty.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Queen's Kitchen is a restaurant that serves classic British, Welsh, and Irish dishes. Its chef can make bowls of cawl and plates of flummery according to the production possibilities curve (PPC) shown here.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4449658d67064e59eeb05a1d238b15e65789e73e)

**Based on the production possibilities curve shown here, what is the opportunity cost of a plate of flummery when the chef goes from producing 10 plates of flummery to 11 plates of flummery?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
